ExtraItemFlags.brief: A set of bit flags defining various special attributes, visual effects, and restrictions for game items.
ExtraItemFlags.details: The ExtraItemFlags group encompasses a comprehensive collection of constants that specify diverse properties, effects, and restrictions applicable to items within the game system. These flags include visual indicators such as glowing and humming effects, physical characteristics like transparency and non-metallic composition, and functional attributes such as container status, immutability, and special behaviors like decay or melting. Additionally, they encode gameplay-related properties including alignment restrictions, cursed or blessed states, anti-good/evil attributes, and quest-related designations. Organized as bit flags, these constants allow for efficient combination and checking of multiple item attributes, supporting complex item interactions, visual cues, and game mechanics that enhance gameplay depth, consistency, and performance.
ExtraItemFlags.notes: The flags are designed to be combined using bitwise operations, enabling flexible assignment of multiple attributes to a single item. Certain flags, such as ITEM_COMPARTMENT and ITEM_NOREMOVE, are critical for game mechanics involving item containment and integrity. The naming conventions and grouping facilitate quick checks and modifications, ensuring consistent behavior across different game systems.
ExtraItemFlags.rationale: This group exists to provide a unified and efficient way to represent a wide array of item properties, effects, and restrictions. By encapsulating these attributes as bit flags, the system can perform rapid evaluations and modifications, supporting complex gameplay mechanics, visual effects, and rule enforcement. This organization enhances code maintainability, scalability, and performance, ensuring that item behaviors are both flexible and consistent within the overall game architecture.
ExtraItemFlags.name: ExtraItemFlags

ITEM_GLOW.brief: The ITEM_GLOW constant is a flag indicating that an object has a glowing visual effect.
ITEM_GLOW.details: ITEM_GLOW is a bit flag within the ExtraItemFlags namespace that signifies whether an object should be rendered with a glow effect. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::A, enabling efficient combination with other flags through bitwise operations. This flag is utilized across various functions to determine if an object should appear to glow, affecting visibility checks, descriptive formatting, and visual effects during enchantments. Its consistent application ensures that glowing items are visually distinguished, improving user experience and clarity in the game interface.
ITEM_GLOW.notes: Since ITEM_GLOW is stored as a bit flag, multiple visual or attribute flags can be combined within a single variable, allowing for scalable and efficient attribute management.
ITEM_GLOW.rationale: The ITEM_GLOW flag exists to provide a clear visual indicator for glowing items, which can be important for gameplay mechanics, item identification, and aesthetic effects. It supports features like highlighting enchanted or special items and ensures consistent visual cues across different game systems.
ITEM_GLOW.name: ITEM_GLOW

usages.act__info_8cc, String format_obj_to_char: Checked to determine if the object has a glowing status and adds corresponding visual indicator.

ITEM_HUM.brief: A constant flag indicating whether an item is currently humming.
ITEM_HUM.details: ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_HUM is a bit flag used to represent the humming state of an item within the system. It is initialized to the bit defined by Flags::B, enabling it to be combined with other item flags through bitwise operations for efficient status management. This flag is commonly checked in functions responsible for generating item descriptions or handling item behaviors, such as appending a humming indicator when applicable. Its design allows for rapid status checks and consistent handling of the humming attribute across various components of the application.
ITEM_HUM.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within a larger set of item attributes, facilitating compact storage and fast bitwise operations for status evaluation.
ITEM_HUM.rationale: The ITEM_HUM flag exists to efficiently represent and check the humming state of items, supporting dynamic item descriptions and behaviors that depend on whether an item is actively humming or producing sound.
ITEM_HUM.name: ITEM_HUM

usages.act__info_8cc, String format_obj_to_char: Checked to determine if the object is humming and adds the corresponding indicator.

ITEM_COMPARTMENT.brief: A bit flag that designates an object as a compartment or container within the game.
ITEM_COMPARTMENT.details: The ITEM_COMPARTMENT constant is a specific bit flag used to identify objects that function as compartments or containers, enabling characters to store, access, or interact with items within them. It is initialized to the same bit position as Flags::C, replacing the previous ITEM_DARK flag to better represent container functionality. This flag is checked across various functions such as examination, object initialization, and container management to determine whether an object should be treated as a compartment. Its consistent application ensures correct behavior in visibility, interaction, and containment logic, facilitating efficient handling of container objects in the game environment.
ITEM_COMPARTMENT.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within the object's attribute set, allowing for efficient bitwise operations. Its replacement of ITEM_DARK indicates a shift in how object states are managed, emphasizing container status over darkness.
ITEM_COMPARTMENT.rationale: The ITEM_COMPARTMENT flag exists to clearly distinguish objects that serve as containers, supporting game mechanics related to item storage and interaction. By using a dedicated bit flag, the system can efficiently check and modify container status, ensuring consistent behavior across various game functions.
ITEM_COMPARTMENT.name: ITEM_COMPARTMENT

ITEM_TRANSPARENT.brief: A bit flag indicating that an item is transparent, allowing visibility through it.
ITEM_TRANSPARENT.details: The ITEM_TRANSPARENT constant is a specific bit within the Flags::Bit enumeration, initialized to the bit represented by Flags::D. It is used to mark items that are see-through, influencing rendering and perception logic within the application. This flag replaces the previous ITEM_LOCK flag in certain contexts, reflecting a shift in how transparency is represented and managed. It is primarily checked during visibility calculations, such as in functions like do_look, to determine if an object obstructs or permits line-of-sight, thereby affecting descriptions and interactions.
ITEM_TRANSPARENT.notes: The flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, enabling efficient storage and quick bitwise operations for attribute checks.
ITEM_TRANSPARENT.rationale: This constant exists to support the game's visibility mechanics by clearly indicating which items are transparent, facilitating accurate perception and rendering behaviors.
ITEM_TRANSPARENT.name: ITEM_TRANSPARENT

usages.act__info_8cc, void do_look(Character *c: Used to decide if an object allows seeing through or into it, affecting descriptions.

ITEM_EVIL.brief: A constant representing the 'evil' attribute flag used to mark objects and entities as evil.
ITEM_EVIL.details: The ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_EVIL constant is a bit flag that signifies an entity or object has an evil alignment or nature within the system. It is initialized with the specific bit value defined by Flags::E, enabling efficient storage and rapid bitwise operations for setting, checking, and removing the evil attribute. This flag plays a crucial role in various game mechanics, such as visual effects, spell interactions, and permission checks, by providing a standardized way to identify evil entities. Its usage across multiple functions ensures consistent handling of evil attributes throughout the codebase.
ITEM_EVIL.notes: The flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, allowing multiple attributes to be combined efficiently. Its use in conjunction with other flags facilitates complex attribute management while maintaining performance.
ITEM_EVIL.rationale: The ITEM_EVIL flag exists to support game mechanics that depend on an entity's moral alignment, such as detecting evil items, applying or removing curses, and modifying spell effects based on alignment. It provides a clear and efficient method to represent and evaluate the evil attribute within the system.
ITEM_EVIL.name: ITEM_EVIL

ITEM_INVIS.brief: A constant flag indicating that an object or character is invisible within the system.
ITEM_INVIS.details: The `ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_INVIS` constant signifies the invisibility status of game objects or characters. It is initialized with the `Flags::F` bit, which is part of a bitfield designed for efficient storage and management of multiple boolean attributes. This flag is used throughout the codebase in functions that apply, check, or remove invisibility effects, such as spells or perception checks. By leveraging bitwise operations, the system can quickly determine whether an entity is invisible and handle interactions accordingly, supporting gameplay mechanics related to stealth and perception.
ITEM_INVIS.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within a larger set of attribute flags, allowing multiple states to be represented compactly and checked efficiently.
ITEM_INVIS.rationale: The constant exists to provide a standardized way to mark entities as invisible, facilitating consistent handling of invisibility effects and perception logic across various game systems.
ITEM_INVIS.name: ITEM_INVIS

ITEM_MAGIC.brief: A constant representing the 'magic' attribute flag for items.
ITEM_MAGIC.details: The ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_MAGIC constant is a bit flag used to denote that an item has magical properties within the system. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::G, which corresponds to a specific position in the Flags::Bit enumeration, allowing for efficient storage and quick bitwise operations. This flag is checked in various functions to determine if an item is magical, such as when displaying item descriptions, applying spell effects, or during enchantment processes. It is also set when an item is enchanted or modified to possess magical qualities, facilitating consistent management of item attributes across different parts of the codebase. The use of bitwise flags enables multiple attributes to be stored compactly and checked rapidly, supporting scalable attribute management for items.
ITEM_MAGIC.notes: This flag is stored within an item's attribute bitfield and is typically combined with other flags to represent multiple properties simultaneously. Its assignment to Flags::G indicates its position within the bitmask, ensuring compatibility with other attribute flags.
ITEM_MAGIC.rationale: The ITEM_MAGIC flag exists to provide a clear, efficient way to mark items as magical, supporting game mechanics such as spell effects, enchantments, and magical detection. It allows the system to quickly identify and handle magical items, ensuring consistent behavior and simplifying attribute management.
ITEM_MAGIC.name: ITEM_MAGIC

ITEM_NODROP.brief: A constant representing the 'no drop' flag for objects, indicating they cannot be dropped by characters.
ITEM_NODROP.details: The ITEM_NODROP constant is a bit flag used within the game's object attribute system to mark items that players or characters are not allowed to drop. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::H, which corresponds to a specific bit position in the Flags::Bit enumeration. This flag is checked in various game functions to enforce restrictions on dropping, teleporting, or disarming objects, ensuring game rules regarding non-droppable items are consistently applied. The flag integrates with the broader bitwise flag system, allowing efficient checks and modifications of object attributes. Its usage is integral to maintaining game balance and preventing players from discarding or manipulating certain key items.
ITEM_NODROP.notes: This flag is stored as a bit within the Flags::Bit enumeration, enabling quick bitwise operations for attribute checks. It is typically combined with other flags to represent multiple object states efficiently.
ITEM_NODROP.rationale: The ITEM_NODROP flag exists to enforce game rules that prevent specific items from being dropped or transferred, preserving item integrity and game balance by restricting player actions on certain objects.
ITEM_NODROP.name: ITEM_NODROP

ITEM_BLESS.brief: A constant representing the 'blessed' status flag for items within the system.
ITEM_BLESS.details: The ITEM_BLESS constant is a bit flag used to designate items as blessed in the application. It is initialized with the Flags::I bit, which is part of a bitmask system designed for efficient storage and quick attribute checks. This flag is commonly used across various functions to determine if an item is blessed, influencing behaviors such as reducing the likelihood of destruction, preventing certain spells like poisoning, and affecting spell effects like removal or detection. Its role is central to the game's item attribute management, enabling rapid and scalable status verification during gameplay interactions.
ITEM_BLESS.notes: The blessed status is stored as a specific bit within an item's attribute flags, allowing multiple attributes to be combined and checked efficiently using bitwise operations.
ITEM_BLESS.rationale: This constant exists to facilitate the identification and handling of blessed items, ensuring consistent behavior across game mechanics such as spell effects, item durability, and detection, thereby supporting the game's design for item attributes and their interactions.
ITEM_BLESS.name: ITEM_BLESS

ITEM_ANTI_GOOD.brief: The constant ITEM_ANTI_GOOD indicates that an item possesses an anti-good property, restricting its use or effects based on alignment.
ITEM_ANTI_GOOD.details: ITEM_ANTI_GOOD is a bit flag defined within the Flags::Bit enumeration, used to mark objects that are incompatible with good-aligned characters or effects. It is initialized with the value Flags::J, representing a specific bit position in a bitfield for efficient storage and evaluation. This flag is utilized across various functions to enforce game mechanics, such as preventing good characters from wearing certain items, influencing spell effects, or determining item drops based on alignment. Its use allows multiple flags to be combined and checked rapidly through bitwise operations, supporting scalable and maintainable code for managing item restrictions related to character alignment.
ITEM_ANTI_GOOD.notes: The flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, enabling efficient combination with other flags for complex attribute management.
ITEM_ANTI_GOOD.rationale: This constant exists to facilitate the enforcement of alignment-based restrictions and effects, ensuring that items with anti-good properties behave consistently within the game's mechanics and interactions.
ITEM_ANTI_GOOD.name: ITEM_ANTI_GOOD

ITEM_ANTI_EVIL.brief: The constant ITEM_ANTI_EVIL is a bit flag that marks objects as anti-evil, restricting their use based on character alignment.
ITEM_ANTI_EVIL.details: ITEM_ANTI_EVIL is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, used to designate objects that are considered anti-evil. It is initialized with the value Flags::K and is employed throughout the codebase to enforce alignment-based restrictions, such as preventing evil characters from wielding, using, or dropping certain items, and ensuring that only good-aligned characters can acquire or equip them. This flag interacts with various game mechanics, including spell effects, item restrictions, and character actions, to uphold role-playing rules and maintain consistency in alignment-based gameplay. Its presence is checked in functions responsible for object equipping, spell effects, and item drops, thereby influencing game behavior in accordance with character alignments.
ITEM_ANTI_EVIL.notes: The ITEM_ANTI_EVIL flag is stored as a single bit within a bitmask, allowing efficient checks using bitwise operations. Its consistent use across different mechanics helps maintain clear and scalable alignment restrictions.
ITEM_ANTI_EVIL.rationale: This constant exists to facilitate the enforcement of alignment restrictions within the game, ensuring that objects designated as anti-evil behave appropriately by preventing misuse or unauthorized usage by characters of certain alignments, thus supporting role-playing and game balance.
ITEM_ANTI_EVIL.name: ITEM_ANTI_EVIL

ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL.brief: Represents the anti-neutral alignment restriction flag for items.
ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL.details: The ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL constant is a bit flag used to denote that an item is restricted to characters with non-neutral alignments. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::L, corresponding to a specific position within the Flags::Bit enumeration. This flag is checked in various functions to enforce alignment-based restrictions, such as preventing neutral characters from wearing, dropping, or gaining certain items. By combining this flag with others using bitwise operations, the system efficiently manages multiple item attributes and enforces game rules related to character alignment and item interactions.
ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, allowing for efficient storage and quick checks through bitwise operations. It is commonly used alongside other flags to represent multiple item properties simultaneously.
ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL.rationale: The ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL flag exists to enforce game mechanics that restrict neutral characters from using certain items, supporting the game's alignment-based rules and ensuring consistent behavior across item interactions.
ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL.name: ITEM_ANTI_NEUTRAL

ITEM_NOREMOVE.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item cannot be removed once it is set.
ITEM_NOREMOVE.details: The ITEM_NOREMOVE constant is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, initialized with the Flags::M bit, which designates items that are protected from removal or disarming actions. This flag is utilized across various functions to enforce item persistence, ensuring that certain objects—such as enchanted weapons, quest items, or other critical objects—remain in a character's inventory until specific conditions are met, like death or completion of a quest. Its consistent use helps maintain game integrity by preventing accidental or intentional removal of essential items, and it interacts with other status checks to uphold item retention policies within the system.
ITEM_NOREMOVE.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within a bitmask, allowing efficient checks using bitwise operations. Its presence in item attributes ensures quick validation during removal or disarm attempts, contributing to overall system performance and reliability.
ITEM_NOREMOVE.rationale: The ITEM_NOREMOVE flag exists to support game mechanics that require certain items to be permanently retained, preventing their removal through standard actions. This ensures critical items remain available for gameplay purposes, such as maintaining enchantments, quest progression, or preventing exploitation by removing powerful or unique objects.
ITEM_NOREMOVE.name: ITEM_NOREMOVE

ITEM_INVENTORY.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item is stored in a character's inventory.
ITEM_INVENTORY.details: The ITEM_INVENTORY constant is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, used to denote that an object resides in a character's inventory. It is initialized with the value Flags::N, which corresponds to a particular bit position in the bitfield. This flag is utilized throughout the codebase to identify inventory items quickly via bitwise checks, affecting various behaviors such as whether an item can be destroyed, its availability for purchase, or whether it can be disarmed. Its consistent use allows for efficient management of inventory-related attributes and interactions within the system.
ITEM_INVENTORY.notes: The ITEM_INVENTORY flag is typically stored as part of a bitmask, enabling multiple attributes to be combined and checked efficiently using bitwise operations. Its use pattern reflects a design choice to optimize performance and maintainability when handling numerous boolean item properties.
ITEM_INVENTORY.rationale: This constant exists to facilitate quick and reliable identification of inventory items, supporting game mechanics such as item management, purchasing, and interaction restrictions. By using a dedicated bit flag, the system can efficiently handle complex attribute combinations and streamline inventory-related logic.
ITEM_INVENTORY.name: ITEM_INVENTORY

ITEM_NOPURGE.brief: A bit flag indicating that an item should not be purged or destroyed during cleanup or effect routines.
ITEM_NOPURGE.details: The ITEM_NOPURGE constant is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, used to mark objects that must be preserved during various in-game effects and cleanup processes. It is initialized with the Flags::O bit, representing a particular position in a bitmask, enabling efficient storage and rapid checks through bitwise operations. This flag is commonly checked in functions such as shock_effect, cold_effect, acid_effect, fire_effect, and do_purge to determine whether an object is exempt from destruction, ensuring critical items remain intact despite environmental or gameplay effects. Its design facilitates scalable management of object persistence across different game scenarios.
ITEM_NOPURGE.notes: The ITEM_NOPURGE flag is stored as a single bit within a bitmask, allowing multiple flags to be combined efficiently. Its consistent use across various effect functions ensures reliable preservation of designated items during complex interactions.
ITEM_NOPURGE.rationale: This constant exists to provide a straightforward mechanism for preventing the accidental or intentional destruction of important items during game effects and cleanup routines, supporting gameplay stability and item persistence.
ITEM_NOPURGE.name: ITEM_NOPURGE

ITEM_ROT_DEATH.brief: A bit flag that indicates an item should automatically decay and be destroyed after a certain period.
ITEM_ROT_DEATH.details: ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_ROT_DEATH is a constant that represents a specific bit within the Flags::Bit enumeration, used to mark items that are subject to automatic decay. It is initialized with the value Flags::P, which corresponds to a particular bit position within the bitfield. This flag is commonly checked in functions such as make_corpse to determine whether decay timers should be applied to an item, leading to its eventual removal from the game world. By using this flag, the system efficiently manages resource cleanup and maintains game world consistency by removing items that are meant to decay after a set duration.
ITEM_ROT_DEATH.notes: This flag is stored as a bit within a bitfield, allowing multiple attributes to be combined efficiently using bitwise operations. Proper handling ensures that items marked with ITEM_ROT_DEATH are correctly identified for decay processing.
ITEM_ROT_DEATH.rationale: The ITEM_ROT_DEATH flag exists to facilitate automated cleanup of decaying items, ensuring that temporary or expired objects do not persist indefinitely, which helps optimize resource usage and maintain a realistic game environment.
ITEM_ROT_DEATH.name: ITEM_ROT_DEATH

usages.fight_8cc, void make_corpse(Character *c: Checked to set decay timers and flags on objects, and to determine if they should decay and be destroyed.

ITEM_VIS_DEATH.brief: A constant flag indicating whether an object is visible in the context of death events.
ITEM_VIS_DEATH.details: The ITEM_VIS_DEATH constant is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, initialized to the value Flags::Q. It is used to mark objects with the ability to be visible or hidden during death-related visibility checks. This flag is typically set or checked when determining if an object, such as a corpse or death-related item, should be visible to characters after death events. Its proper management ensures that death-related objects are displayed or concealed according to game mechanics, facilitating accurate and consistent visibility behavior in the game environment.
ITEM_VIS_DEATH.notes: This flag is stored as a bit within a bitfield, allowing efficient storage and quick bitwise operations for visibility checks. It is often combined with other flags to represent multiple attributes simultaneously.
ITEM_VIS_DEATH.rationale: The ITEM_VIS_DEATH flag exists to support the game's visibility logic related to death events, ensuring that objects are correctly shown or hidden based on their death status and associated visibility rules.
ITEM_VIS_DEATH.name: ITEM_VIS_DEATH

usages.fight_8cc, void make_corpse(Character *c: Used to remove visibility flags from objects when they are placed into the corpse, affecting their visibility after death.
usages.handler_8cc, bool can_see_obj(const Char: Checked to see if the object has the death visibility flag and if the object is carried by the character, affecting visibility.

ITEM_NOSAC.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item cannot be sacrificed or affected by certain interactions.
ITEM_NOSAC.details: The ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_NOSAC constant is a bit flag used within the system to mark items that should be exempt from sacrificial actions or specific interactions. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::R, representing a particular position in the Flags::Bit enumeration, which allows for efficient bitwise operations. This flag is commonly checked in functions such as acceptable_sac to determine if an item is eligible for sacrifice or other game mechanics, thereby influencing how items are processed and interacted with in various contexts.
ITEM_NOSAC.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, enabling quick checks and compact storage. Its use pattern follows the standard approach of defining item attributes as individual bits for flexible attribute management.
ITEM_NOSAC.rationale: The ITEM_NOSAC flag exists to provide a clear and efficient way to prevent certain items from being sacrificed, ensuring game mechanics respect item-specific restrictions and maintaining consistent behavior across different scenarios.
ITEM_NOSAC.name: ITEM_NOSAC

usages.act__obj_8cc, bool acceptable_sac(Charac: Referenced in the IS_OBJ_STAT macro to determine if the object has the NOSAC status, affecting acceptability.

ITEM_NONMETAL.brief: Represents the 'non-metallic' attribute flag for items, indicating that an item is made of non-metal materials.
ITEM_NONMETAL.details: ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_NONMETAL is a bit flag used to denote that an item is non-metallic within the system. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::S, which corresponds to a specific position in the Flags::Bit enumeration. This flag is employed in bitwise operations to efficiently check or assign the non-metallic property of items, affecting game mechanics such as spell interactions, damage calculations, and other material-dependent behaviors. Its consistent use across functions that evaluate item material properties ensures streamlined and performant handling of item attributes in various contexts.
ITEM_NONMETAL.notes: The flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, allowing multiple attributes to be combined efficiently. Proper use of bitwise operators is essential for accurate checks and modifications.
ITEM_NONMETAL.rationale: This constant exists to facilitate quick and reliable identification of non-metallic items, enabling game mechanics like spells or effects that depend on material properties to function correctly and efficiently.
ITEM_NONMETAL.name: ITEM_NONMETAL

usages.magic_8cc, void spell_heat_metal(skill::: Checked to determine if an object is non-metallic, influencing whether the spell affects it.

ITEM_NOLOCATE.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item cannot be located or targeted by locating spells or effects.
ITEM_NOLOCATE.details: ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_NOLOCATE is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration used to mark items that should be excluded from locating or targeting spells. It is initialized with the value 'Flags::T', which corresponds to a particular bit position in a bitmask, allowing for efficient storage and checks. This flag is utilized in functions such as spell_locate_object and spell_summon_object to determine whether an item can be targeted or summoned, thereby preventing certain items from being locatable or targetable based on game logic. Its use ensures that items meant to remain hidden or protected from locating effects are properly flagged and handled within the system.
ITEM_NOLOCATE.notes: The ITEM_NOLOCATE flag is stored as a single bit within a bitmask, enabling quick bitwise operations for attribute checks. Its consistent use across locating and summoning functions helps maintain game integrity by controlling item visibility and accessibility.
ITEM_NOLOCATE.rationale: This constant exists to provide a clear and efficient way to designate items that should be immune to locating spells or effects, supporting game mechanics that require certain items to remain hidden or protected from detection.
ITEM_NOLOCATE.name: ITEM_NOLOCATE

usages.magic_8cc, void spell_summon_object(skil: Flag checked to exclude objects that are not locatable for summoning.
usages.magic_8cc, void spell_locate_object(skil: Checked via macro IS_OBJ_STAT to skip objects that are marked with the NOLOCATE status, preventing them from being located.

ITEM_MELT_DROP.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item should melt or dissolve when dropped or destroyed.
ITEM_MELT_DROP.details: ITEM_MELT_DROP is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, initialized to the value Flags::U, which represents a unique bit position used for item attribute management. When this flag is set on an item, it signals that the item should automatically melt into dust, smoke, or similar effects upon being dropped or destroyed in the game environment. This behavior is typically checked in functions like do_drop to determine if the item requires special handling for melting effects, enabling dynamic and immersive item interactions. The flag's design leverages efficient bitwise operations for quick attribute checks and modifications across the system.
ITEM_MELT_DROP.notes: The ITEM_MELT_DROP flag is stored as a single bit within a bitfield, allowing multiple item attributes to be combined and checked efficiently. Its initialization to Flags::U ensures it occupies a distinct bit position, avoiding conflicts with other flags.
ITEM_MELT_DROP.rationale: This constant exists to support special item behaviors where items are intended to dissolve upon being dropped or destroyed, enhancing gameplay realism and item interaction dynamics. It provides a clear and efficient way to mark such items within the system.
ITEM_MELT_DROP.name: ITEM_MELT_DROP

usages.act__obj_8cc, void do_drop(Character *ch: Checked to determine if an object should dissolve into dust or smoke upon being dropped or destroyed.",

ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT.brief: A constant representing the ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT flag used to indicate items that should be removed from the game upon sale.
ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT.details: The ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT constant is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, initialized to the value Flags::W. It is used to mark objects that are eligible for extraction from the game world after being sold, aiding in resource management and cleanup. This flag is checked in functions such as get_cost, do_sell, and do_buy to determine whether an item qualifies for extraction, thereby influencing sale and purchase logic and ensuring proper handling of item lifecycle. Its use enables efficient bitwise operations to manage item attributes and sale behaviors consistently across the codebase.
ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within the Flags::Bit enumeration, allowing for compact storage and rapid checks using bitwise operations. Its consistent use across sale-related functions helps maintain predictable item lifecycle management.
ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT.rationale: The ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT flag exists to streamline the process of removing sold items from the game environment, preventing resource leaks and ensuring that items marked for extraction are properly cleaned up after transactions.
ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT.name: ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT

usages.act__obj_8cc, void do_buy(Character *ch,: Used to check if an item can be sold multiple times or is a one-time sale, affecting haggling logic.",
usages.act__obj_8cc, void do_sell(Character *ch: Checks whether the item should be removed from the game after sale, affecting whether extract_obj is called.
usages.act__obj_8cc, int get_cost(Character *ke: Used in a condition to determine if the object has the ITEM_SELL_EXTRACT status, influencing the cost adjustment logic.

ITEM_BURN_PROOF.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item is resistant to burning or fire-related destruction.
ITEM_BURN_PROOF.details: The ITEM_BURN_PROOF constant is a bit flag used to mark items that should be immune to fire damage or destruction. It is initialized with the value Flags::Y, signifying that the burn-proof property is active when this flag is set. This flag is checked in various spells and effects, such as fireproofing spells or environmental hazards, to prevent these effects from damaging or destroying burn-proof items. As part of the Flags::Bit enumeration, it allows for efficient combination and testing of multiple item properties, facilitating flexible and performant item attribute management within the system.
ITEM_BURN_PROOF.notes: This flag is stored as a bit within a bitfield, enabling quick bitwise operations for property checks and modifications.
ITEM_BURN_PROOF.rationale: The constant exists to provide a standardized way to identify items that should be immune to fire-based effects, supporting game mechanics that require certain items to be protected from environmental hazards or spells that could otherwise destroy them.
ITEM_BURN_PROOF.name: ITEM_BURN_PROOF

ITEM_NOUNCURSE.brief: A constant flag indicating that an item is immune to curses.
ITEM_NOUNCURSE.details: ExtraItemFlags::ITEM_NOUNCURSE is a bit flag used within the item attribute system to specify that an item cannot be affected by curses. It is initialized with the bit value Flags::Z, which allows it to be combined with other attribute flags through bitwise operations for flexible item property management. This flag is commonly checked in functions such as spell_remove_curse to prevent curse effects or removal attempts on items marked with ITEM_NOUNCURSE, ensuring that certain items remain unaffected by curses throughout gameplay.
ITEM_NOUNCURSE.notes: The flag is stored as a single bit within the item's attribute bitfield, enabling efficient checks and combinations with other flags.
ITEM_NOUNCURSE.rationale: This constant exists to provide a clear and efficient way to designate items as immune to curses, supporting game mechanics that require certain items to remain unaffected by curse-related effects or spells.
ITEM_NOUNCURSE.name: ITEM_NOUNCURSE

usages.magic_8cc, void spell_remove_curse(skill: Flag checked to determine if the object is cursed and whether removal attempts should proceed.

ITEM_QUESTSELL.brief: A bit flag indicating that an item is designated for quest-related selling.
ITEM_QUESTSELL.details: The constant ITEM_QUESTSELL is a specific bit flag within the Flags::Bit enumeration, used to mark objects that are associated with quest activities, particularly those that can be sold as part of a quest. It is initialized with the value Flags::a, representing a particular bit position within a bitmask. This flag is checked in functions such as get_cost to determine if an item is flagged for quest selling, which influences pricing and transaction logic. Utilizing bit flags enables efficient storage and rapid evaluation of multiple item attributes within a single integer value, facilitating scalable attribute management across the system.
ITEM_QUESTSELL.notes: This flag is stored as a single bit within a bitmask, allowing multiple attributes to be combined and checked efficiently using bitwise operations.
ITEM_QUESTSELL.rationale: The ITEM_QUESTSELL flag exists to clearly identify items that are involved in quest-related transactions, ensuring that such items are handled appropriately during sales and pricing calculations, thereby supporting quest mechanics and item management consistency.
ITEM_QUESTSELL.name: ITEM_QUESTSELL
